tip_title: Most People Guess at Their Life Insurance Number. Here's How to Actually Check It.
topic: how much life insurance coverage do you need
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Most People Guess at Their Life Insurance Number. Here's How to Actually Check It.**

A lot of people picked their life insurance coverage amount at some point in the past and haven't thought about it since. The number made sense then — but your income, your mortgage balance, your kids' ages, and your household expenses have probably shifted quite a bit since you first signed up. The basic framework most advisors use: figure out how many years your family would need your income replaced, multiply that by your annual earnings, then add your outstanding debts and any big future costs (college, for example) on top. If your current policy number doesn't come close to that figure, it's worth a conversation. This is especially relevant if you bought a term policy years ago when coverage amounts were smaller and your financial picture looked different.

Not sure if your coverage still fits where you are today? Your Peachy agent can help you think through the math — no pressure, just a real look at the numbers.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/insurance/life/learn/how-much-life-insurance-do-i-need)
summary: Covers how to evaluate whether your current life insurance coverage amount is still adequate, using an income-replacement and debt-addition framework — focused on rechecking an existing policy amount rather than the decision to purchase life insurance.
scoring_notes: Both audiences have high income and meaningful homeownership — life insurance adequacy is relevant across the board. Prior life insurance tips covered beneficiary review (June 14) and marriage-triggered policy review (June 18). This tip takes a distinct angle: not who gets the money, not what life events trigger a review, but whether the dollar amount of coverage you currently have still makes sense given income, debt, and future costs. Genuinely fresh angle. East Cobb audience skews 42 median age with college-age kids, making the 'future costs + outstanding mortgage' framing very resonant. Source is nerdwallet.com, which is on the trusted list. Evergreen but paired well with hurricane tip to diversify categories.

tip_title: Hurricane Season Started June 1. Here's the One Insurance Step Most People Skip.
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Hurricane Season Started June 1. Here's the One Insurance Step Most People Skip.**

Atlantic hurricane season runs June 1 through November 30, and the peak months are still ahead of us. Most people think about emergency kits and plywood — but the insurance side of prep gets ignored until something actually happens. The step worth taking right now is pulling out your declarations page and checking three things: whether your dwelling coverage still reflects what it would cost to rebuild at today's prices, whether you have a separate wind or hurricane deductible (and if so, what it is), and whether you have any flood coverage at all — because standard homeowners policies don't include it. None of this takes more than fifteen minutes, and finding a gap now is a whole lot better than finding it during a claim.

Not sure what you're looking at on your dec page? Your Peachy agent can walk through it with you before the busy part of the season hits.

📖 [Learn more from the Insurance Information Institute](https://www.iii.org/press-release/be-financially-prepared-for-hurricane-season-with-the-iiis-new-insurance-checklist-041615)
summary: Covers hurricane season insurance prep focused on three declaration page checks: dwelling coverage adequacy, wind/hurricane deductible existence, and whether flood coverage is in place — framed as a proactive pre-storm review rather than post-event damage coverage.
scoring_notes: June 22 — we are 3 weeks into hurricane season with peak months ahead. Both audiences are high-homeowner-rate (78%+), making dwelling and flood coverage checks directly actionable. Prior water damage tip (April 2026) covered rain vs. flood framing; this tip takes a different angle — it's a proactive pre-storm insurance checklist review (dwelling rebuild cost, hurricane deductible awareness, flood coverage gap) rather than explaining how flood damage is categorized. No overlap with prior hail deductible tip either — that was percentage-based deductible mechanics, not hurricane prep. Trusted source: iii.org. Strong seasonal urgency.

tip_title: Just Got Married? Your Insurance Situation Probably Just Changed.
topic: Marriage and Life Insurance Policy Review
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Just Got Married? Your Insurance Situation Probably Just Changed.**

Marriage is one of those life events that quietly reshapes a bunch of financial decisions at once, and insurance is near the top of that list. Two people, two cars, possibly two homes becoming one — that combination almost always means there's overlap, gaps, or just flat-out outdated policies sitting around. Health insurance is a big one: marriage is a qualifying life event, which means you have a window to add a spouse to your plan or compare whether separate policies or a combined one makes more sense. But don't stop there. Auto, home or renters, and life coverage all need a second look when two households merge into one.

Not sure what needs updating? Your Peachy agent can do a quick rundown with you and flag anything that needs attention before it becomes a problem.

📖 [Learn more from Forbes Advisor](https://www.forbes.com/advisor/life-insurance/life-insurance-for-married-couples/)
summary: Covers how marriage triggers a review of multiple insurance policies (health, auto, home, life), including the qualifying life event window for health insurance and the need to consolidate or update coverage when two households merge.
scoring_notes: June is peak wedding season, making this highly timely for both newsletters. Both audiences have meaningful family and life-event skews. Not a repeat — prior life insurance tip focused narrowly on beneficiary designations; this tip covers the broader post-marriage policy review across multiple coverage types. Actionable immediately for anyone recently married or with a kid who just got married (relevant for the East Cobb empty-nester and established-family mix). Forbes Advisor is a trusted source.

tip_title: One Water Coverage Most Homeowners Don't Know Is Missing From Their Policy
topic: Homeowners Insurance - Sewer Backup and Water Endorsements
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: One Water Coverage Most Homeowners Don't Know Is Missing From Their Policy**

Here's something a lot of homeowners find out the hard way: a standard homeowners policy typically does NOT cover damage from a backed-up sewer or drain. That's a separate endorsement you have to add. It matters more in the summer because heavy rain puts extra stress on municipal sewer systems — and when those systems get overwhelmed, the flow can reverse back into your home through floor drains, toilets, or basement pipes. The repair and cleanup costs from that kind of event can run into the thousands, and without the endorsement, you're paying it out of pocket. It's usually one of the cheaper add-ons available, but you have to know to ask for it.

Not sure if your policy has sewer backup coverage? Your Peachy agent can check your declarations page with you in about five minutes.

📖 [Learn more from the U.S. News & World Report](https://www.usnews.com/insurance/homeowners-insurance)
summary: Covers sewer and drain backup as a coverage gap in standard homeowners policies, the summer-season relevance of heavy rain overwhelming sewer systems, and how adding a backup endorsement closes that gap.
scoring_notes: June in Georgia means heavy rain, flash flooding, and stressed sewer infrastructure — sewer backup is directly timely. 78% homeownership rate for East Cobb makes this highly relevant. Prior water damage tip (April) focused on the rain vs. flood distinction and flood insurance; this is a genuinely different angle: sewer/drain backup as a specific endorsement gap within a standard policy, not flood insurance at all. Actionable: readers can call their agent today to check. Source (U.S. News) is widely trusted for insurance content.

tip_title: The Liability Number on Your Home Policy Might Be Lower Than You Think
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: The Liability Number on Your Home Policy Might Be Lower Than You Think**

Most standard homeowners policies include somewhere between $100,000 and $300,000 in personal liability coverage. That sounds like a lot until you think about what it actually has to cover — a guest who trips and breaks their arm, a tree that falls on a neighbor's car, a dog bite that leads to a lawsuit. Medical bills and legal fees add up faster than most people expect, and if a judgment comes in above your policy limit, the difference can come out of your own pocket. If you have meaningful assets to protect (a paid-down home, retirement accounts, college savings), it's worth pulling up your declarations page and checking what that liability number actually says.

Not sure if your current limit makes sense for where you are financially? Your Peachy agent can walk through the options with you in about ten minutes.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/insurance/personal-liability-insurance-homeowners)
summary: Covers personal liability limits in standard homeowners policies (typically $100K-$300K), why those limits may be insufficient for asset-rich households, and when an umbrella policy makes sense to fill the gap.
scoring_notes: Strong fit for both audiences — high homeownership rates (78%), above-average incomes ($118K median), meaning these households have real assets to protect and are exactly the profile where a default $100K-$300K liability limit is underdone. June is peak outdoor-entertaining and pool season in Georgia, which is exactly when liability exposure spikes (guests, kids, gatherings). Not a repeat subject — liability coverage limits and umbrella have not appeared in the prior 6 months. NerdWallet is a trusted source per skill guidelines.

tip_title: The Liability Limit Buried in Your Home Policy Might Not Be Enough Anymore
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: The Liability Number on Your Home Policy Is Worth a Second Look**

Standard homeowners and renters policies come with a personal liability limit — usually somewhere between $100,000 and $300,000 — that covers you if someone gets hurt on your property or you're held responsible for damage to someone else's stuff. That number gets set when you first buy a policy and often just... stays there. The problem is that a single lawsuit, a serious injury on your property, or a significant accident can push well past those limits, and anything above your coverage limit comes out of your own pocket.

If your assets — home equity, savings, retirement accounts — have grown since you last looked at that number, it's worth asking whether your liability limit has kept pace. An umbrella policy is one option that extends liability coverage to $1 million or more and typically costs a few hundred dollars a year.

Not sure what your current liability limit is or whether it still makes sense? Your Peachy agent can pull it up and walk you through the options.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/insurance/personal-liability-insurance-homeowners)
summary: Covers personal liability limits on homeowners and renters policies — specifically the risk that the default $100,000-$300,000 limit is set-and-forgotten and may not keep pace with growing personal assets, and how umbrella coverage extends those limits.
scoring_notes: High household incomes across all three newsletters ($95k-$118k) with meaningful homeowner populations make personal liability limits a strong relevance fit — more assets means more exposure above a standard limit. Renters in the Perimeter and Lewisville Lake audiences are served by the 'renters policy also has a liability section' framing. Evergreen topic but genuinely not covered in the prior 6 months — the category is umbrella/liability which has had zero prior coverage in the dedup list. Diversifies away from the home-property tips that have run frequently. Source is nerdwallet.com, which is on the trusted list.

tip_title: Storm Season Is Here — That Wind/Hail Deductible on Your Policy Isn't What You Think
topic: wind and hail damage home insurance
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Your Wind and Hail Deductible Probably Isn't a Flat Number**

Most people assume their deductible is the flat dollar amount they see on their auto policy. On homeowners policies, wind and hail damage often works differently. A lot of policies have a separate wind/hail deductible that's calculated as a percentage of your home's insured value, not a fixed dollar amount. On a home insured for $400,000 with a 2% wind deductible, that's $8,000 out of pocket before insurance touches a claim from a summer storm — which catches a lot of homeowners off guard when they're standing in the driveway looking at a roof full of dents.

Pull up your declarations page and look for a line item that says something like "windstorm deductible" or "hail deductible." If it shows a percentage instead of a dollar figure, do the math on what that actually means for your home's insured value.

Not sure how to read your declarations page? Your Peachy agent can walk through it with you in about ten minutes.

📖 [Learn more from The Zebra](https://www.thezebra.com/resources/home/separate-deductible-for-wind-and-hail/)
summary: Covers how wind and hail deductibles on homeowners policies are often percentage-based rather than flat dollar amounts, and what that means for out-of-pocket costs on a storm claim. Angle is understanding the deductible structure, not whether coverage exists.
scoring_notes: June storm season makes this extremely timely for Georgia (hail and thunderstorms peak May-August) and North Texas (severe hail season also peaks spring-summer). Both newsletters have meaningful homeowner populations (78% East Cobb, 55% Perimeter, 65% Lewisville Lake). The prior hurricane-prep tip (May 3) covered windstorm deductibles briefly in a hurricane context — this tip's distinct angle is the percentage-based deductible math specifically for wind/hail, which is a separate and underappreciated mechanic that applies even to non-hurricane storms. Source is thezebra.com, which is not on the trusted list, but the content is factual, structural, and corroborated by the NAIC and III. Best available source for this specific angle. Not a carrier recommendation, no premium figures, no scare tactics.

tip_title: Your Phone Might Be Worth a Discount on Your Car Insurance
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Your Phone Might Be Worth a Discount on Your Car Insurance**

Usage-based insurance, aka UBI or telematics, lets your carrier measure how you actually drive instead of just using your age and zip code to set your rate. Things like smooth braking, avoiding late-night driving, and keeping mileage low can LOWER your premium over time. You typically opt in through an app or a small plug-in device, and many programs give you a discount just for signing up. If you drive less than average or consider yourself a careful driver, it's worth asking whether your policy has a telematics option.

Curious whether a usage-based program makes sense for your situation? Your Peachy agent can walk you through how those programs typically work.

📖 [Learn more from Bankrate](https://www.bankrate.com/insurance/car/usage-based-insurance/)
summary: Explains how usage-based (telematics) auto insurance programs work, covering how driving behavior and mileage data can lower premiums, and encourages drivers who log fewer miles or drive carefully to ask about opt-in programs.
scoring_notes: Auto tip diversifies the category mix from the home-business pick above. Relevant across all three audiences: Perimeter young professionals often have shorter commutes or WFH days reducing mileage; East Cobb empty nesters may drive less than when kids were young; Lewisville Lake mix includes families with multiple vehicles. Not previously covered. Bankrate is a trusted source in the skill's list (comparable consumer-finance authority; note: it's not on the explicit trusted list but is a well-regarded source -- flagging this for review). Actionable in a concrete 'ask your agent' way. Timeliness is evergreen but appropriate for a month where summer driving patterns shift. Soft seasonal hook: summer road trips mean people are thinking about auto coverage anyway.
